findings.title: OECD Pillar Two GIR central filing deadline: June 30, 2026
findings.summary: The OECD released a Common Understanding on GIR Central Filing with 33 jurisdictions confirmed as ready for central filing. Calendar-year taxpayers benefit from an 18-month first-year extension making June 30, 2026 the first filing deadline for Global Information Returns. Jurisdictions may waive local filing penalties where a GIR is centrally filed in one of the listed jurisdictions.
findings.client_impact: MNE clients subject to Pillar Two with calendar-year accounting periods must file or ensure their GIR is filed centrally by June 30, 2026 — missing this deadline risks local filing penalties in each jurisdiction where the group operates. Clients headquartered in the 33 participating jurisdictions (including France, Germany, Ireland, Luxembourg, Switzerland, and the UK) can satisfy local filing obligations via central filing but must act immediately.
findings.urgency_reason: Hard statutory deadline 22 days from today (June 30, 2026); first-ever GIR filing for calendar-year MNEs with no further extensions announced.

findings.title: BVI entities that missed January 1, 2026 BOI filing deadline are now 'In Penalty'
findings.summary: All BVI entities incorporated before January 2, 2025 were required to file beneficial ownership information with the Registrar of Corporate Affairs by January 1, 2026. Entities that missed the deadline are flagged 'In Penalty' on the BVI FSC system. New entities must file within 30 days of incorporation via the VIRRGIN online platform. The filing fee is USD 125.
findings.client_impact: Any HNW client with a BVI entity that did not file BOI by January 1, 2026 is currently in active penalty status that can impair the entity's good standing, restrict its ability to open or maintain bank accounts, and signal compliance failures to counterparties. Immediate filing via VIRRGIN is required to restore good standing.
findings.urgency_reason: Entities are already in active penalty status as of January 2, 2026; each additional day of non-compliance compounds risk to entity good standing and banking relationships.

findings.title: SEC Reg S-P cybersecurity compliance deadline of June 3, 2026 for smaller RIAs has passed
findings.summary: Smaller investment advisers (AUM below $1.5 billion) were required to comply with SEC Regulation S-P amendments by June 3, 2026, including written incident response programs, 30-day client notification requirements for cybersecurity incidents, 72-hour vendor notification requirements, and expanded recordkeeping. The SEC has designated Reg S-P compliance an examination priority for 2026. The rule now covers RIAs advising solely private funds.
findings.client_impact: FGC and any RIA clients with AUM below $1.5 billion are now five days past the compliance deadline and subject to examination findings and enforcement if the required written policies, procedures, and notification protocols are not yet in place; remediation must be treated as an immediate operational priority.
findings.urgency_reason: Compliance deadline passed June 3, 2026 — five days ago. The SEC has explicitly listed Reg S-P as a 2026 examination priority, making non-compliant firms immediate enforcement targets.

findings.title: LC 227/2026: Brazil raises ITCMD ceiling to 16% and authorizes taxation of foreign assets from January 1, 2027
findings.summary: Enacted January 13, 2026, LC 227/2026 mandates all Brazilian states adopt progressive ITCMD (inheritance and gift tax) rates up to a 16% federal ceiling. The law closes a longstanding loophole by authorizing taxation of inheritances and donations involving assets located abroad held by Brazilian residents, and requires equity to be valued at market value rather than net book value. Major changes, including the foreign asset provisions, are broadly effective January 1, 2027.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident HNW clients with offshore trusts, foreign foundations, or overseas investment accounts will for the first time face state-level ITCMD on gifts and inheritances involving those foreign assets from January 1, 2027; succession planning structures should be reviewed before year-end to assess exposure and identify restructuring options, which can take months to implement.
findings.urgency_reason: Major foreign-asset provisions take effect January 1, 2027, giving approximately seven months to plan; restructuring options such as trust redomiciliation or ownership changes require lead time and should not be deferred.

findings.title: BCB Resolution 521: stablecoins treated as foreign exchange operations with mandatory monthly reporting from May 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolution No. 521/2025 treats stablecoins as foreign exchange operations and requires monthly submission of detailed counterparty information to the Banco Central for capital controls and AML purposes. Transaction limits are set at USD 100,000 per transaction for pure VASPs and USD 500,000 for FX-authorized financial institutions. Reporting obligations became effective May 2026.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident clients using stablecoins for cross-border transfers are now subject to FX reporting rules; transactions above applicable limits may be restricted and each transaction must be reported to the BCB with counterparty identification, creating a paper trail with direct tax and exchange-control compliance implications.
findings.urgency_reason: Reporting obligations are already in force as of May 2026; clients with existing stablecoin transaction flows must immediately verify their reporting compliance and transaction limit adherence.

findings.title: FinCEN BOI reporting now applies only to foreign-formed entities registered in the US
findings.summary: An Interim Final Rule revised the definition of 'reporting company' to include only entities formed under foreign law and registered to do business in a US state or tribal jurisdiction. All US-formed entities and their beneficial owners are now fully exempt. Foreign companies registered before March 26, 2025 had an April 25, 2025 deadline; those registered on or after March 26, 2025 have 30 days from US registration to file.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using foreign-formed holding companies or SPVs registered in any US state (e.g., a Cayman or BVI entity qualified to do business in Florida) must file BOI with FinCEN within 30 days of registration and update filings within 30 days of any ownership change; US LLCs and corporations formed domestically are now fully exempt.
findings.urgency_reason: Rule is in force on a rolling basis; any new foreign entity registration in a US state triggers an immediate 30-day filing clock, and clients with foreign entities registered in the US after March 26, 2025 may have open obligations.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 foreign buyer restrictions upheld; mandatory closing affidavit for all buyers remains in force
findings.summary: Florida's SB 264 (effective July 1, 2023), restricting real estate purchases by nationals from seven 'countries of concern' (China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria), was upheld by courts in November 2025. All buyers of Florida real property must sign an affidavit at closing under penalty of perjury. Chinese nationals face a near-total ban; other affected nationals face restrictions primarily on agricultural land and property near critical infrastructure. FIRPTA 15% withholding continues to apply separately.
findings.client_impact: Clients who are nationals of the seven restricted countries must be screened before any Florida real estate transaction; transactions by prohibited nationals can result in criminal penalties and forced divestiture, making pre-transaction nationality analysis essential for all Florida deals involving foreign clients.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is in permanent force after judicial validation in November 2025; ongoing compliance risk for every new Florida real estate transaction involving potentially affected nationals.

findings.title: Cayman Islands implements CRS 2.0 and CARF effective January 1, 2026: crypto-assets now reportable
findings.summary: The Cayman Islands Tax Information Authority amended CRS Regulations effective January 1, 2026, implementing OECD CRS 2.0. Crypto-assets, e-money, and CBDCs are now within CRS reporting scope. New registration deadlines applied (April 30, 2026 one-time deadline for entities that became FIs during 2025). CRS Return and Compliance Form are both due by June 30 from 2027 onward. FIs must implement written due diligence policies at account opening.
findings.client_impact: Cayman-domiciled funds, SPVs, and family office structures that hold or transact in crypto-assets must now report those holdings and transactions under CRS to the TIA for onward exchange with client home countries; clients who assumed crypto-asset accounts were outside automatic exchange of information must reassess their domestic disclosure positions immediately.
findings.urgency_reason: New reporting scope is in force from January 1, 2026; FIs administering Cayman structures must update client onboarding, classification, and annual reporting procedures without delay.

findings.title: CIMA requires Cayman-resident Principal Point of Contact for registered funds by January 31, 2027
findings.summary: Effective January 1, 2026, CIMA amendments require that the Principal Point of Contact for all registered mutual and private funds be a Cayman Islands resident. Entities registered before January 1, 2026 without a local PPoC must comply by January 31, 2027. Annual fees have been consolidated, replacing separate annual and return fees.
findings.client_impact: Funds registered before 2026 without a Cayman-resident PPoC must appoint one — typically via a licensed fund administrator or corporate service provider — before January 31, 2027; failure to comply risks regulatory action by CIMA, and the engagement process takes weeks.
findings.urgency_reason: Compliance deadline is January 31, 2027 (approximately eight months away); appointing a qualifying PPoC requires engaging a licensed Cayman service provider and should be initiated now to avoid year-end congestion.

findings.title: BVI Legitimate Interest Access to beneficial ownership register operational since April 1, 2026
findings.summary: The BVI Financial Services Commission launched its Legitimate Interest Access system on April 1, 2026. Applicants demonstrating a legitimate AML or counter-financing-of-terrorism purpose can access information on beneficial owners holding 25% or more, including name, birth month and year, nationality, and nature of interest. A non-refundable fee of USD 75 applies with a 12-day processing timeline.
findings.client_impact: BVI structures are no longer fully private: counterparties, regulators, and creditors with a demonstrable AML or due diligence purpose can now obtain partial beneficial ownership information; clients who relied on BVI confidentiality as a structuring objective should revisit their privacy assumptions and ensure their structures are defensible.
findings.urgency_reason: System is already operational; privacy exposure exists now and requires immediate reassessment for any client where BVI confidentiality was a key consideration.

findings.title: Luxembourg new carried interest tax regime broadens eligibility and offers preferential tax rates from tax year 2026
findings.summary: Luxembourg's new carried interest regime, effective tax year 2026, broadens eligibility beyond employees to include independent board members, consultants, and others providing services to AIFMs. Contractual carried interest is taxed as extraordinary income at one-quarter of the global tax rate with no holding period required. Participation-linked carried interest qualifies for capital gains treatment with exemption available after six months where the holding is 10% or less.
findings.client_impact: Clients who are fund managers, board members, or consultants to Luxembourg AIFMs can now access materially reduced tax rates on carried interest entitlements from 2026; those who previously structured carried interest arrangements outside Luxembourg should assess whether redomiciliation or restructuring to capture this regime is beneficial.
findings.urgency_reason: Regime applies to tax year 2026; planning decisions made this year will determine tax treatment — clients should review existing arrangements before year-end to maximize benefit.

findings.title: Luxembourg AIFMD II (Bill 8628) in force April 16, 2026: new delegation, liquidity management, and loan origination rules
findings.summary: Luxembourg's AIFMD II implementing law entered into force April 16, 2026. Key changes include: delegation requirements extended to fund administration and other Annex I activities; AIFMs managing open-ended AIFs must select at least two liquidity management tools from an EU-harmonised list; a comprehensive loan origination regime with concentration limits and leverage caps; prohibition on Luxembourg AIFs appointing foreign EU depositaries; and enhanced reporting obligations from April 16, 2027.
findings.client_impact: Clients invested in or managing Luxembourg-domiciled AIFs must confirm that their fund's governance documents, liquidity risk management framework, and service provider arrangements comply with the new rules; the April 16, 2027 enhanced reporting deadline requires early system and documentation preparation.
findings.urgency_reason: Law is in force now; funds must audit compliance with delegation and liquidity requirements immediately and begin preparation for the enhanced reporting regime ahead of the April 2027 deadline.

findings.title: FINMA consultation on revised AMLO-FINMA closes June 9, 2026; final ordinance expected end-2026
findings.summary: On May 12, 2026, FINMA launched a consultation on a partial revision of the AMLO-FINMA to prevent violations of coercive measures under the Embargo Act and align with updated FATF recommendations. The consultation period closes June 9, 2026. The revised ordinance reinforces requirements to understand client structure and clarifies obligations for payable-through accounts and sub-accounts held for individuals.
findings.client_impact: Swiss-regulated banks, trust companies, and asset managers used by FGC's clients will face tightened AML documentation and embargo-screening requirements once the revised ordinance enters force later in 2026; clients with complex ownership structures or Swiss payable-through accounts should anticipate enhanced due diligence requests from Swiss counterparts.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation closes June 9, 2026 (tomorrow); the final ordinance is expected before year-end and will directly affect KYC burden on Swiss institutions servicing HNW clients.

findings.title: Switzerland Federal Transparency Act (TLEA) requiring centralized UBO register expected in force autumn 2026
findings.summary: Parliament passed the Federal Act on the Transparency of Legal Entities and Identification of Beneficial Owners (TLEA) in September 2025. Entry into force is expected in autumn 2026. Over 500,000 Swiss companies and foreign entities with Swiss nexus (branches, effective management, or Swiss real property) must register beneficial owners — defined as natural persons holding 25%+ of capital or voting rights, or exercising equivalent control — via EasyGov. The register will be restricted to criminal prosecution, tax, SECO, and MROS authorities and will not be publicly accessible.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Swiss AGs, GmbHs, foundations, associations, or foreign entities with Swiss branches or real property will need to file UBO information before the statutory deadline; the scale of the registry (500,000+ entities) means advisers and service providers will be overwhelmed near the deadline, so early preparation is strongly advisable.
findings.urgency_reason: Entry into force is expected in approximately three to four months; registration infrastructure is still being finalized and clients should begin UBO mapping and governance documentation now.

findings.title: MAS tightens Section 13O/13U family office tax incentive conditions from May 2026
findings.summary: MAS extended both the Section 13O and Section 13U family office tax incentive schemes to December 31, 2029 but tightened conditions from May 2026. The applicable minimum AUM must now be maintained for the entire life of the fund, not just at year-end. Local Business Spending requirements scale with fund AUM under a 2026 tiered framework. Investment professionals must earn a minimum fixed monthly salary of SGD 3,500 and be Singapore tax-resident. Processing timelines are four to nine months from a complete application.
findings.client_impact: Existing Singapore family offices approved under 13O or 13U must verify compliance with the continuous AUM threshold requirement; any period where AUM drops below the minimum — even temporarily — could jeopardize the incentive and result in full corporate tax liability on previously exempt investment income.
findings.urgency_reason: Tightened conditions are in force from May 2026; family offices that previously managed AUM tactically at year-end must update their monitoring frameworks and investor reporting immediately.

findings.title: MAS designates AML/CFT enhancements for the wealth management sector as 2026 examination priority
findings.summary: MAS introduced AML/CFT enhancements applicable to the full asset and wealth management sector, including Capital Markets Services licensees, Variable Capital Companies, trust companies, and capital markets intermediaries. Key requirements include proliferation financing risk assessments, expanded due diligence for trust and legal arrangements, shorter timelines for suspicious transaction reporting, and elevated Source of Wealth verification standards.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients accessing wealth management services through Singapore-regulated entities should expect significantly more rigorous Source of Wealth documentation requests and longer onboarding timelines; clients with complex structures involving trusts, foundations, or opaque legal arrangements will face the most heightened scrutiny.
findings.urgency_reason: MAS has designated these as 2026 examination priorities, meaning Singapore-based managers and trustees are enforcing enhanced procedures now; clients with outdated SOW documentation risk account restrictions.

findings.title: DIFC opens Participant Company regime to all global applicants; non-compliant existing entities have six months to appoint a CSP
findings.summary: DIFC is removing qualifying requirements for its Participant Company regime, making it globally accessible. Most PCs will be required to appoint a DFSA-regulated corporate service provider. Non-compliant existing entities have six months from the May 2026 announcement to comply or face fines up to USD 20,000 and potential loss of PC status.
findings.client_impact: Clients with DIFC Participant Companies that do not currently have a DFSA-regulated CSP must appoint one before approximately November 2026; failure results in financial penalties and may trigger loss of PC status, which could unwind beneficial tax and structuring positions.
findings.urgency_reason: Six-month compliance window runs from May 2026, implying a deadline of approximately November 2026; CSP engagement and DIFC regulatory approval take time, so clients should begin immediately.

findings.title: UAE FTA Public Clarification CTP008 sets out corporate tax treatment of family wealth management structures
findings.summary: The UAE Federal Tax Authority published Public Clarification CTP008 on the corporate tax implications of family wealth management structures, covering Family Foundations, holding companies, SPVs, Single Family Offices, and Multi-Family Offices. Passive family wealth structures meeting Article 17 conditions can remain outside UAE Corporate Tax scope. Ministerial Decision No. 261 of 2024 broadened Article 17 to include subsidiaries wholly owned by a tax-transparent Family Foundation through an uninterrupted chain. Family Offices are treated as Taxable Persons and must charge arm's-length fees to related parties.
findings.client_impact: UAE-based or UAE-structured HNW family offices must confirm their structures satisfy the Article 17 conditions to remain outside corporate tax scope; in particular, any Family Office charging below arm's-length fees to related parties creates a corporate tax exposure, and multi-tier structures must trace ownership to a qualifying Family Foundation.
findings.urgency_reason: CTP008 is now the operative guidance for UAE corporate tax compliance of family structures; clients who structured prior to this clarification should validate their arrangements before their next UAE corporate tax filing.

findings.title: ADGM finalizes virtual asset staking framework and tightens AML rules; UAE cabinet tax penalty changes effective April 2026
findings.summary: ADGM's FSRA finalized a regulatory framework for staking of virtual assets, including tighter rules for firms staking client assets, building on its 2025 public consultation. ADGM is also aligning its AML framework with updated UAE AML laws and FATF standards. UAE Cabinet penalty changes for tax law violations took effect April 14, 2026, introducing a simplified, compliance-encouraging penalty structure across all UAE taxes.
findings.client_impact: Clients using ADGM-licensed virtual asset service providers to stake digital assets are now subject to a formal regime imposing client-asset protection and reporting obligations; additionally, the revised UAE tax penalty structure may reduce penalties for early voluntary disclosure of past tax non-compliance.
findings.urgency_reason: Staking framework and AML enhancements are in force now; clients with ADGM virtual asset relationships should confirm their VASP's compliance status and review staking agreements against the new client-asset protection requirements.

findings.title: Panama SBP Acuerdo 1-2026 raises UBO disclosure threshold and tightens AML documentation for all SBP-supervised entities
findings.summary: Acuerdo No. 01-2026, published January 2026, applies to all SBP-supervised entities and significantly raises AML/CFT documentation requirements. Key changes include: full UBO transparency to individuals holding 10% or more; certified documentation for all beneficial owners holding 25% or more; mandatory inferential geolocation for digital account opening; and enhanced requirements for economic activity evidence and source of funds at account opening and throughout the client relationship.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Panama bank accounts or SBP-supervised structures should anticipate enhanced due diligence requests including certified documentation of all beneficial owners and source-of-funds evidence; clients with nominee structures or indirect beneficial ownership chains will face the most significant impact.
findings.urgency_reason: Acuerdo 1-2026 is already in force; SBP-supervised banks will apply these requirements to existing clients at the next scheduled review and to all new account openings immediately.

findings.title: Jersey Companies Law modernization in force June 1, 2026; JFSC raises UBO reporting threshold to 25%
findings.summary: The Companies (Jersey) Amendment Law 2026 entered into force June 1, 2026, modernizing share capital, distributions, mergers, director duties and indemnities, shareholder meetings, audit requirements, and winding-up. Separately, as of March 31, 2026, the JFSC raised the beneficial ownership reporting threshold in low-risk entity registration applications from 10% to 25%, aligning with international standard practice.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Jersey companies benefit from greater structural flexibility in areas such as distributions and director indemnities, but should have their constitutional documents reviewed against the updated law; the raised UBO threshold reduces disclosure burden for certain low-risk applications but does not change reporting obligations for regulated entities.
findings.urgency_reason: Law came into force one week ago on June 1, 2026; existing Jersey companies should have their governance documents reviewed promptly to confirm compliance and capture available structural flexibilities.

findings.title: Cyprus comprehensive tax reform effective January 1, 2026: CIT raised to 15%, DDD abolished, dividend WHT cut to 5%
findings.summary: Cyprus enacted a comprehensive tax reform effective January 1, 2026. Corporate Income Tax increased from 12.5% to 15% to align with OECD Pillar Two. The Deemed Dividend Distribution rule was abolished, allowing companies and funds to retain profits without artificial tax leakage. Withholding tax on actual dividends was reduced to 5%. Additional changes include stamp duty abolition, extended loss carry-forward, and a new anti-abuse rule for transactions with blacklisted or low-tax jurisdictions. From 2031, gains from redemption of fund units in collective investment schemes will be treated as dividends.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Cyprus holding companies or fund structures face a higher headline CIT rate but benefit materially from DDD abolition — eliminating prior annual deemed distribution charges — and the reduced 5% dividend WHT; the overall tax efficiency of Cyprus structures should be remodelled under the new regime to determine whether existing structuring assumptions remain valid.
findings.urgency_reason: Changes are in force for the 2026 tax year; clients filing Cyprus corporate tax returns or making dividend distributions in 2026 are operating under the new rules and must confirm their structures are optimised.

findings.title: Isle of Man Financial Services (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill introduced March 2026; civil penalties regime in consultation
findings.summary: The Isle of Man Financial Services Authority introduced the Financial Services (Miscellaneous Provisions) Bill to Tynwald on March 31, 2026 to enhance regulatory and oversight frameworks in line with updated international standards. Certain measures were removed or deferred following consultation feedback. Secondary legislation on a new civil penalties regime will follow a further consultation in 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Isle of Man-registered entities should monitor the progress of this Bill, as the forthcoming civil penalties regime will increase enforcement risk for non-compliance with IoM financial services requirements once enacted; the final scope of penalties will be determined through the secondary consultation expected later in 2026.
findings.urgency_reason: Bill is at an early legislative stage with no confirmed enactment date; ongoing monitoring required as the civil penalties framework develops through 2026.
findings.source_name: Isle of Man Financial Services Authority

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two Side-by-Side Package: four new safe harbors agreed January 5, 2026; US MNEs exempt
findings.summary: On January 5, 2026, the OECD Inclusive Framework agreed the Pillar Two Side-by-Side Package via new Administrative Guidance, including four new safe harbors. The Side-by-Side Safe Harbour exempts MNE Groups headquartered in jurisdictions with eligible domestic tax regimes from IIR and UTPR charges in other jurisdictions. The US Treasury confirmed US-headquartered MNEs are exempt from Pillar Two requirements. Work on replacing two aspects of the transitional safe harbor was expected to conclude by June 2026.
findings.client_impact: MNE clients headquartered in a jurisdiction with an eligible domestic tax regime may qualify for the Side-by-Side Safe Harbour, potentially eliminating top-up tax exposure in other jurisdictions; US-headquartered groups are fully exempt from Pillar Two, which is directly relevant to any clients considering group restructuring decisions based on Pillar Two exposure.
findings.urgency_reason: Safe harbors are available for the current tax year; clients should assess eligibility as part of 2026 Pillar Two compliance, particularly given the June 30, 2026 GIR filing deadline.

findings.title: CRS 2.0 / CARF global rollout: UK and EU bring digital assets into automatic exchange from 2026
findings.summary: Multiple jurisdictions implemented OECD CRS 2.0 and CARF effective January 1, 2026. The UK brought digital wallets, e-money accounts, and CBDCs into CRS scope and will discontinue the combined CRS/FATCA reporting schema after December 31, 2026, requiring FIs to use new schemas from 2027. The EU's DAC8 aligns CRS with CARF to cover digital assets across EU member states. The Cayman Islands also implemented CRS 2.0 and CARF from January 1, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding crypto-assets, e-money accounts, or CBDCs through UK, EU, or Cayman financial institutions will have those holdings automatically reported to their home-country tax authority for the 2026 tax year; clients who have not made domestic disclosures of such holdings face significant tax and penalty exposure as data begins arriving in home countries.
findings.urgency_reason: Automatic reporting of digital assets is in effect for 2026; clients with undisclosed crypto or e-money holdings should seek tax advice immediately before their home-country tax authority receives the first CARF reports.

findings.title: BCB VASP authorization framework fully in force from February 2, 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolutions 519, 520, and 521 established a comprehensive VASP regulatory regime requiring prior BCB authorization. VASPs must hold between R$10.8 million and R$37.2 million in capital depending on their activities. A 270-day grandfathering period for existing VASPs expired and the framework is now fully operative. Algorithmic stablecoins are expressly prohibited.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Brazilian crypto exchanges or VASPs should confirm their provider holds current BCB authorization; unregistered VASPs face enforcement action and clients transacting through them risk loss of funds and regulatory scrutiny.
findings.urgency_reason: Framework is fully in force; no immediate deadline, but clients should perform a one-time confirmation of their Brazilian VASP providers' authorization status.

findings.title: FinCEN Investment Advisers AML/BSA rule effective date delayed to January 1, 2028
findings.summary: FinCEN issued a final rule extending the effective date of the Investment Advisers Anti-Money Laundering rule from January 1, 2026 to January 1, 2028, providing additional compliance runway for affected entities. The AML Regulations for Residential Real Estate Transfers Rule was also postponed to March 1, 2026.
findings.client_impact: RIAs and investment advisers preparing for the January 2026 AML compliance date now have until January 2028; however, pausing compliance work entirely is inadvisable as the rule will ultimately take effect and advance preparation reduces operational disruption.
findings.urgency_reason: Delay is confirmed; no immediate action required. Advisers should maintain compliance programs rather than dismantle work already completed.

findings.title: FIRPTA withholding rates unchanged in 2026: 15% standard rate continues to apply
findings.summary: No legislative changes to FIRPTA in 2026. The standard 15% withholding rate continues to apply to foreign persons disposing of US real property interests. The reduced 10% rate applies to residential properties sold for $1 million or less. Sellers may apply for a withholding certificate via Form 8288-B to reduce withholding based on actual tax liability.
findings.client_impact: Foreign clients selling US real property remain subject to 15% FIRPTA withholding on gross proceeds; clients expecting a lower actual tax liability should file Form 8288-B prior to closing to avoid over-withholding and the administrative burden of claiming a refund.
findings.urgency_reason: Status quo confirmed for 2026; reference information for any US real estate transaction involving foreign sellers.

findings.title: Cayman CRS annual return and Compliance Form deadline moved from July 31 to June 30, effective for 2026 reporting year
findings.summary: As part of CRS 2.0 amendments effective January 1, 2026, Cayman Islands financial institutions will be required to file both the CRS Return and the CRS Compliance Form by June 30 (previously July 31) of the year following the reporting period. NIL returns are also subject to the new deadline. The change first applies to the 2026 reporting year, with returns due by June 30, 2027.
findings.client_impact: Cayman-domiciled funds and financial institutions should update their compliance calendars to reflect the earlier June 30 deadline starting in 2027; the change gives administrators approximately one month less to prepare annual CRS filings.
findings.urgency_reason: Administrative deadline change only; next affected deadline is June 30, 2027, providing sufficient lead time for calendar updates.

findings.title: Luxembourg Start-Up Investment Tax Credit available from tax year 2026
findings.summary: Bill 8526, adopted December 17, 2025, allows individuals investing in qualifying young innovative companies to claim a new tax credit from tax year 2026. The credit applies to investments made from 2026 onward and is part of Luxembourg's broader 2026 tax package, which also increases social security contributions.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg-resident or Luxembourg-taxpaying clients making qualifying investments in young innovative companies from 2026 may be eligible for a tax credit; this is potentially relevant for clients building venture or private equity portfolios through Luxembourg structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Opportunity is available from 2026 tax year; no urgent deadline, but relevant for current-year investment planning decisions in Luxembourg.

findings.title: UAE new capital markets framework (Federal Decree-Laws) in force January 1, 2026
findings.summary: New Federal Decree-Laws effective January 1, 2026 repealed the previous Securities and Commodities Authority framework and introduced a comprehensive statute-driven regime governing UAE onshore capital markets, covering all securities issuance, trading, and intermediation activities.
findings.client_impact: Clients issuing securities, trading, or operating as intermediaries in UAE onshore capital markets are now governed by the new Capital Market Authority framework; existing participants should confirm their licenses and compliance arrangements are aligned with the new regulatory structure.
findings.urgency_reason: Framework is established and in force; background reference for clients with UAE onshore capital markets activities.

findings.title: GFSC updates AML/CFT/CPF Handbook and publishes enforcement actions, May 2026; Crown Dependency Quad Forum established
findings.summary: The Guernsey Financial Services Commission published updates to its AML/CFT/CPF Handbook on May 8, 2026 and issued enforcement public statements against several trust professionals. The GFSC and its Crown Dependency counterparts (Jersey, Isle of Man, Gibraltar) established a 'Quad Island Forum' to integrate tax authority intelligence for financial crime prevention. Russia and Taliban sanctions notices were also updated in May 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Guernsey trust or fund structures should be aware that the GFSC is actively enforcing its AML handbook and now coordinates with Jersey, Isle of Man, and Gibraltar tax authorities; any compliance gaps in Guernsey structures should be reviewed against the updated handbook.
findings.urgency_reason: Background intelligence; signals active GFSC enforcement posture and cross-jurisdiction intelligence sharing that increases detection risk for non-compliant structures.

findings.title: Securities Commission of the Bahamas consults on draft Securities Industry Regulations 2026
findings.summary: The Securities Commission of the Bahamas issued draft Securities Industry Regulations 2026 for public consultation, proposing to repeal and replace the Securities Industry Regulations 2012. The draft covers all major Bahamas licensing regimes including the Securities Industry Act 2024, Investment Funds Act 2019, Financial and Corporate Service Providers Act 2020, and the Digital Assets and Registered Exchanges Act 2024.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Bahamas-registered funds, broker-dealers, or digital asset exchanges should monitor this consultation as the final regulations will modernize the compliance and licensing framework; substantial changes to reporting, capital, and conduct requirements are possible.
findings.urgency_reason: Early-stage public consultation; final regulations not yet published. No immediate client action required — monitor for developments.

findings.title: MFSA launches consultation on tokenisation of financial instruments and real-world assets; deadline June 30, 2026
findings.summary: The Malta Financial Services Authority launched a public consultation on the tokenisation of financial instruments and real-world assets, exploring how DLT can be incorporated into Malta's regulatory landscape. Industry members may submit comments until June 30, 2026. The consultation aims to identify operational challenges, regulatory barriers, and viable use cases for a proportionate regulatory framework.
findings.client_impact: Clients interested in issuing or holding tokenised securities or real assets through Malta structures should monitor this consultation as it will shape Malta's regulatory framework for digital asset tokenisation; no immediate compliance obligation exists, but the outcome may open new structuring options.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation paper stage with a June 30, 2026 submission deadline; framework changes are expected but not yet enacted — background awareness for clients exploring Malta-based digital asset structures.
